evidence_quotes.quote: I remember spending a lot of money on a videogame, then later was "cancelled" by the publisher, all the money sunk into it was lost.

Moreover, there were a few games that i have bought, a few games that i have paid for my hard earned money for, that are at risk of being just erased from stores, erased from existance, not to be accessible anymore.
I find this kind of behaviour even more appaling than overabundance of lootboxes.

paragraphs.text: Hey. I'm a concerned gamer. I remember spending a lot of money on a videogame, then later was "cancelled" by the publisher, all the money sunk into it was lost. Moreover, there were a few games that i have bought, a few games that i have paid for my hard earned money for, that are at risk of being just erased from stores, erased from existance, not to be accessible anymore. I find this kind of behaviour even more appaling than overabundance of lootboxes. Of course, i understand that everyone who's making software doesn't do it for the fun of it - especially when it comes to things as expensive as modern videogames - but unlike physical product that you buy once and can use for all eternity, or untill you utterly and irrevocably break it - this kind of product is at the mercy of publishers and execs, who care for nothing but the profit. It is my firm belief that video games are a form of art, and art should be preserved, regardless of the whims of CEOs. Thank you for coming to my TED talk
